analogy: Think of "Peasants Zamindars and the State" through the chapter's own opening images: THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 196 During the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries about 85 per cent of the population of India lived in its villages. Both peasants and landed elites were involved in agricultural production and claimed rights to a share of the... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Peasants Zamindars and the State is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 196 During the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries about 85 per cent of the population of India lived in its villages.

The text explains that both peasants and landed elites were involved in agricultural production and claimed rights to a share of the produce.

The text explains that this created relationships of cooperation, competition and conflict among them.

The text explains that the sum of these agrarian relationships made up rural society.

The text explains that at the same time agencies from outside also entered into the rural world.

The text explains that most important among these was the Mughal state, which derived the bulk of its income from agricultural production.

The text explains that agents of the state – revenue assessors, collectors, record keepers – sought to control rural society so as to ensure that cultivation took place and the state got its regular share of taxes from the produce.

The text explains that since many crops were grown for sale, trade, money and markets entered the villages and linked the agricultural areas with the towns.

The text explains that peasants and Agricultural Production The basic unit of agricultural society was the village, inhabited by peasants who performed the manifold seasonal tasks that made up agricultural production throughout the year – tilling the soil, sowing seeds, harvesting the crop when it was ripe.

The text explains that further, they contributed their labour to the production of agro-based goods such as sugar and oil.

The text explains that but rural India was not characterised by settled peasant production alone.

The text explains that several kinds of areas such as large tracts of dry land or hilly regions were not cultivable in the same way as the more fertile Fig.

The text explains that 8.1 A rural scene Detail from a seventeenth-century Mughal painting Peasants, Zamindars and the State A A A A Ag g g g gr r r r rar ar ar ar arian Socie ian Socie ian Socie ian Socie ian Society and t ty and t ty and t ty and t ty and the Mughal Em he Mughal Em he Mughal Em he Mughal Em he Mughal Empir pir pir pir pire e e e e ((((( c c c c c. sixteent . sixteent . sixteent . sixteent . sixteenth h h h h ----- seventeenth centuries) seventeenth centuries) seventeenth centuries) seventeenth centuries) seventeenth centuries) THEME EIGHT

The text explains that in addition, forest areas made up a substantial proportion of territory.

The text explains that we need to keep this varied topography in mind when discussing agrarian society.

The text explains that 1.1 Looking for sources Our understanding of the workings of rural society does not come from those who worked the land, as peasants did not write about themselves.

The text explains that our major source for the agrarian history of the sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries are chronicles and documents from the Mughal court (see also Chapter 9).

The text explains that fortunately, however, the account of the Ain can be supplemented by descriptions contained in sources emanating from regions away from the Mughal capital.

The text explains that all these sources record instances of conflicts between peasants, zamindars and the state.

The text explains that 1.2 Peasants and their lands The term which Indo-Persian sources of the Mughal period most frequently used to denote a peasant was raiyat (plural, riaya) or muzarian.

The text explains that sources of the seventeenth century refer to two kinds of peasants – khud-kashta and pahi-kashta.

The text explains that source 1 Ü Describe the aspects of agricultural life that struck Babur as particular to regions in northern India.

The text explains that what kind of resources would each of these systems require?

The text explains that 8.2 A reconstructed Persian wheel, described here Source 2 PEASANTS, ZAMINDARS AND THE STATE

The text explains that we often come across the term jins-i kamil (literally, perfect crops) in our sources.

The text explains that though there was no census at that time, the little data that we have suggest that such groups comprised a large section of the village population, had the least resources and were constrained by their position in the caste hierarchy, much like the Dalits of modern India.

The text explains that there was a direct correlation between caste, poverty and social status at the lower strata of society.

The text explains that some sources suggest that the headman was chosen through the consensus of the village elders, and that this choice had to be ratified by the zamindar.

The text explains that the relatively easy availability of uncultivated land and the competition over labour resources made this an effective weapon in the hands of cultivators.

The text explains that some British officials in the nineteenth century saw the village as a “little republic” made up of fraternal partners sharing resources and labour in a collective.

The text explains that with the growth of nucleated villages and expansion in individuated peasant farming, which characterised medieval Indian agriculture, the basis of production was the labour and resources of the entire household.

The text explains that women were considered an important resource in agrarian society also because they were child bearers in a society dependent on labour.

The text explains that though it is nearly impossible to set an all-India average of the forest cover for this period, informed conjectures based on contemporary sources suggest an average of 40 per cent.

The text explains that source 3 Ü What forms of intrusion into the forest does the text evoke?

The text explains that 8.10 A peasant and a hunter listening to a sufi singer Source 4 Ü What are the modes of transport described in this passage?

The text explains that the Zamindars Our story of agrarian relations in Mughal India will not be complete without referring to a class of people in the countryside that lived off agriculture but did not participate directly in the processes of agricultural production.

The text explains that control over military resources was another source of power.

The text explains that contemporary documents give an impression that conquest may have been the source of the origin of some zamindaris.

The text explains that more important were the slow processes of zamindari consolidation, which are also documented in sources.

The text explains that for instance, in 1665, Aurangzeb expressly instructed his revenue officials to prepare annual records of the number of cultivators in each village (Source 7).

The text explains that source 5 Ü What principles did the Mughal state follow while classifying lands in its territories?

The text explains that 215 The jama This is an excerpt from Aurangzeb’s order to his revenue official, 1665: He should direct the amins of the parganas that they should discover the actual conditions of cultivation ( maujudat), village by village, peasant-wise ( asamiwar), and after minute scrutiny, assess the jama, keeping in view the financial interests ( kifayat) of the government, and the welfare of the peasantry.

The text explains that source 6 The Ain on land revenue collection: Let him (the amil-guzar) not make it a practice of taking only in cash but also in kind.

The text explains that the Flow of Silver The Mughal Empire was among the large territorial empires in Asia that had managed to consolidate power and resources during the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.

The text explains that source 7 Ü Why do you think the emperor insisted on a detailed survey?

The text explains that this was good for India as it did not have natural resources of silver.

The text explains that the Ain gives detailed accounts of the organisation of the court, administration and army, the sources of revenue and the physical layout of the provinces of Akbar’s empire and the literary, cultural and religious traditions of the people.

The text explains that 219 Source 9 “Moistening the rose garden of fortune” In this extract Abu’l Fazl gives a vivid account of how and from whom he collected his information: ... to Abu’l Fazl, son of Mubarak … this sublime mandate was given. “Write with the pen of sincerity the account of the glorious events and of our dominion-conquering victories … Assuredly, I spent much labour and research in collecting the records and narratives of His Majesty’s actions and I was a long time interrogating the servants of the State and the old members of the illustrious family.

The text explains that by these means, I constructed a reservoir for irrigating and moistening the rose garden of fortune (the Akbar Nama). Ü List all the sources that Abu’l Fazl used to compile his work.

The text explains that which of these sources would have been most useful for arriving at an understanding of agrarian relations?

The text explains that what are the problems in using the Ain as a source for reconstructing agrarian history?

The text explains that huntington, The Art of Ancient India, Weatherhill, New York, 1993.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History II, Chapter 4: Peasants Zamindars and the State

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
